input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control hardware. Its hardware hierarchy comprises a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and autonomy elements (perception, planning, etc.). The vehicle operates in a driving scenario where Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) is exercised, making it suitable for digital‑twin studies because it combines safety‑critical control loops, resource‑constrained ECUs, and rich sensor data that can be monitored and off‑loaded.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three services: (1) Data Monitoring – a real‑time user interface visualises vehicle state variables, projected trajectory, and detected object tracks, and enables users to save data and generate reports; (2) Computation Off‑loading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, distance‑based speed computation) runs on cloud resources rather than the on‑board ECU, with the resulting desired speed streamed back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI lets operators enable/disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue emergency‑brake commands over a low‑latency communication channel. These services target operators (monitoring and control) and developers (troubleshooting and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a custom graphical user interface for visualization and control, cloud‑based compute resources to host the off‑loaded ACC algorithm, and a low‑latency end‑to‑end communication link between the vehicle and the Digital Twin to meet safety‑critical timing constraints. Integrated data‑collection and reporting modules support the monitoring and remote‑management capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires that all devices share a common notion of time to enable computation offloading; clock synchronization must achieve 100 ms accuracy (limited by hardware), while many safety‑critical vehicular standards mandate tighter synchronization, indicating that the digital twin operates on a sub‑second time granularity with a synchronization interval of at most 100 ms.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The architecture instantiates three top‑level entities—Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity—each deployed on a separate hardware component. This results in a decentralized, single‑instance deployment per entity, with no indication of multiple twin instances or hierarchical nesting beyond the entity level.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, organizing software components into Functional Entities (FEs) that are implemented as individual ROS 2 packages containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. The Physical Twin Entity hosts resource‑specific functionality (e.g., point‑cloud clustering), the Digital Twin Entity provides data management and control, and the User Entity contains the User Interface FE split into visualization and control panel. This entity‑based design orchestrates the system through ROS 2 communication and ROS‑based component integration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The ISO 23247 framework allows Functional Entities to be implemented with standards such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA), suggesting that external data exchange and interoperability with other enterprise systems can be achieved via OPC UA. No additional external integration patterns or protocols are detailed in the provided documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system includes a Data Storage functional entity that aggregates object tracks and the ego‑vehicle state into a relational SQL database. Computational models are implemented as ROS2 nodes within the Digital Twin Entity: a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component ingests raw LiDAR point clouds, clusters them using the DBSCAN algorithm, and maintains object tracks with a Kalman‑filter based state estimator. These models constitute geometric data (raw point clouds), behavioral models (Kalman filter tracking), and analytical processing (DBSCAN clustering). The model outputs are stored in the SQL database for later retrieval by the User Interface.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is realized through a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller that receives desired speed commands from the Digital Twin and converts them into motor voltage commands driving the vehicle’s propulsion motor, as well as a steering servo that adjusts wheel angle. Control commands are exchanged via ROS2 topics, enabling remote actuation from the User Interface. The actuation range is limited by the motor and servo capabilities, and safety constraints are imposed by the speed controller’s firmware (not detailed in the document).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing layer consists of a Hokuyo UST‑10LX 2‑D LiDAR and an Intel DeepSense D435i depth camera mounted on the vehicle. The LiDAR provides distance measurements used by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, while the depth camera supplies RGB‑D data for additional perception tasks. Sensor data are streamed to ROS2 nodes at the devices’ native sampling rates and forwarded to the Physical Twin Entity for preprocessing. Precise sampling frequencies and data rates are not specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point clouds, vehicle state, motor, steering and servo signals) is streamed to the Digital Twin System (DTS) where a Data Pre‑Processing Front‑End (Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) transforms raw LiDAR points into object tracks and a projected driving path. All data streams are passed through the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE, which serialises the data, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Within the DTS the software components exchange data over a ROS 2 middleware network, while communication between the physical and digital entities occurs over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. The documents do not specify transmission frequencies, event triggers, or explicit data‑quality assurance mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The DTS runs the ACC service, computes the lead‑vehicle identification and desired speed, and sends the desired speed command back to the vehicle where an Actuation Front‑End (Motor, Steering, Servo) executes the actuation. The Remote Management UI can also transmit enable/disable ACC commands, set‑point speed adjustments and emergency‑braking commands to the physical twin. These control messages are conveyed over the same Wi‑Fi UDP channel used for sensor data, but the documents do not detail the command payload format, validation, authentication, or acknowledgement protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS intra‑component communication relies on a ROS 2 network, providing publish/subscribe messaging among software modules. Inter‑entity (physical‑digital) communication uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP as the transport protocol. The system is required to support low end‑to‑end latency communication to satisfy safety‑critical ACC operation, though specific latency, bandwidth, reliability, redundancy or security protocol details are not provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin’s compute workload (e.g., the ACC algorithm, data collection, reporting and visualization services) is off‑loaded from the resource‑constrained vehicle to external compute resources described as “cloud” or other off‑vehicle platforms. These platforms host ROS 2 nodes for the various Front‑Ends (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) and provide storage for collected vehicle state and sensor data. Deployment can thus be distributed, with the physical twin on‑board the vehicle and the digital twin services on remote servers or edge devices capable of running the required ROS 2 components. While the documents mention the need for scalability and low latency, they do not specify concrete hardware models, storage capacities, scalability mechanisms, redundancy strategies, or availability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The digital twin is engineered by first enumerating key software components (see Table I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators, then mapping each component onto a Functional Entity (FE) defined by the ISO 23247 reference architecture. Each FE is implemented as an independent ROS 2 package; a package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that realize the FE’s functionality. The User‑facing functionality is organized in the User Entity, where the User Interface FE is split into a 3‑D visualization sub‑component and a control‑panel sub‑component. Data‑management and control logic reside in the Digital Twin Entity, while data pre‑processing, actuation and device‑communication logic are placed in the Physical Twin Entity. The design acknowledges a deviation from the standard by introducing a distinct Physical Twin Entity that hosts general automotive resources (e.g., point‑cloud clustering and tracking). No explicit statements about quality‑assurance, testing, version‑control or evolution management are provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is provided through the User Interface FE, which is realized by a 3D visualization component and a control‑panel component. These UI elements expose real‑time state data of the Physical Twin (e.g., sensor readings, computed point‑cloud clusters) and allow the operator to issue remote commands that are forwarded to the Physical Twin Entity for actuation. The system therefore enables basic monitoring‑driven decisions, but no advanced analytics, predictive insights, or confidence measures are described.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system adopts the ISO 23247 digital‑twin framework for manufacturing, specifically part 2 (Reference Architecture), to structure the twin into Observable Manufacturing Elements, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity, each composed of Functional Entities. The implementation follows the ISO‑defined entity‑based approach and aligns software components with the corresponding functional entities. Communication and interoperability may employ OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as suggested in the standard, although the actual deployment uses ROS 2 packages for intra‑twin communication.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: Accurately describes the 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle and its hardware hierarchy (lower/upper chassis, autonomy elements) as seen in Fig. 1. Mentions ACC scenario and suitability for digital twinning, but adds 'resource-constrained ECUs' not explicitly stated in snippets. Supported by quotes: 'The physical twin (PT) is a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle', 'physical architecture consists of three layers... lower level chassis... upper level chassis... autonomy elements'.

Excellent coverage of the three services (Data Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) with precise details matching the source text. Correctly identifies target users and capabilities. Supported by quotes: 'Data Monitoring: ...user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'Computation Offloading: ...offloading only the ACC system from the vehicle to the DT', 'Remote Management: ...enable/disable ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command'.

Correctly identifies custom GUI, cloud compute, low-latency communication, and data-collection/reporting modules. However, misses specific technologies mentioned in the source like ROS2, NVIDIA Jetpack SDK, and Wi-Fi network. Supported by quotes: 'UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'allocate compute to better-suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud', 'low end-to-end latency communication'.

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the temporal constraints defined in the requirements section, specifically the 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy (R8) driven by the need for computation offloading and shared time. It also correctly references the 100 ms network latency requirement (R7). However, the extraction slightly misstates 'accuracy' as a 'synchronization interval', though the sub-second granularity is correctly inferred.

The description precisely matches the architectural design described in Section IV.B, which identifies three top-level entities (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity). It correctly notes that each entity is deployed on a separate hardware component and accurately describes the system as a decentralized, single-instance deployment based on the case study context.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and specific summary of the system's orchestration. It correctly identifies the mapping of ISO 23247 Functional Entities to individual ROS 2 packages containing nodes. It accurately assigns specific responsibilities to each entity (e.g., point-cloud clustering in the Physical Twin Entity, data management in the Digital Twin Entity, and the split UI in the User Entity) as detailed in Section IV.B and Table I.

The extraction correctly identifies the mention of OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) in the context of the ISO 23247 framework for implementing Functional Entities (Data Translation FE), inferring its potential for interoperability. It also correctly notes that the provided documents do not detail other specific external integration protocols or patterns, as the implementation relies on a custom ROS 2 and Wi-Fi network.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the data storage mechanism and the computational models. It correctly states that data is stored in a SQL database, quoting 'Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database' from Table I. It details the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component, supported by the quote 'Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter' from Table I. The classification of models into geometric, behavioral, and analytical is a logical synthesis of the text describing point clouds, tracking, and clustering.

The description is specific and well-supported by the source text. It correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and 'steering servo' from Section III-B. The conversion of speed commands is supported by Table I: 'Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands'. The mention of ROS2 topics aligns with the architecture description in Section IV-B ('Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network'). The note about firmware safety constraints being undetailed is accurate based on the provided text.

The extraction correctly lists the 'Hokuyo UST-10LX 2-D LiDAR' and 'Intel DeepSense D435i depth camera' as found in Section III-B. It accurately describes the LiDAR's role in the tracking component, supported by Table I ('Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds'). The mention of ROS2 data streaming is consistent with Section III-B ('ROS2 as the middleware'). The explicit statement that sampling rates are not in the document is correct and avoids speculation.

The description states 'Not in Document'. While the Introduction generally defines Digital Twins as 'real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections', the document does not specify model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error correction mechanisms specific to the case study implementation. The only requirements listed (R7, R8) concern network latency and clock synchronization, not model fidelity or validity. Therefore, 'Not in Document' is correct.

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**
